LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE AUG-22-2018 HEARING ON DEFENDANTS DIGNITY HEALTH, INC. AND ST. FRANCIS MEMORIAL HOSPITAL'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. THE MOTION IS GRANTED. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR COMPLETE LANGUAGE OF THE ADOPTED TENTATIVE RULING.) COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANTS IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH COMPLIES WITH CCP 437C(G) AND REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING AND MUST BE IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: HAROLD E. KAHN, CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR 8600, 415.551.5868, EMAIL: TORREANOM@AOL.COM. (302/HEK)
(DUPLICATIVE DISPOSITION CODE FOR PURPOSES OF ENTRY OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY.)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Amended)
Off Calendar Aug-22-2018 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:
Defendants Dignity Health, Inc. and St. Francis Memorial Hospital's motion for summary judgment. (Part 2 of 2 of the Court's ruling)

Defendants told Ms. Santiago to apply for leave and provide documentation or she would be considered absent without leave. Ms. Santiago failed to do so and her employment was terminated. While Ms. Santiago shows that she attempted to apply for leave and did not do so because of a misunderstanding or miscommunication, the law is settled that a non-discriminatory error in judgment by an employer is not discrimination. To show that an employer's proffered reason for termination of an employee's employment is pretextual, an employee "cannot simply show that the employer's decision was wrong or mistaken, since the factual dispute at issue is whether discriminatory animus motivated the employer, not whether the employer is wise, shrewd, prudent, or competent."' (Hersant v. Department of Social Services (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 997, 1005.) Moreover, Ms. Santiago impermissibly relies on her third amended complaint in an attempt to create a triable issue regarding pretext. (Defendants' Undisputed Material Facts 38 and 75.) Ms. Santiago's fourth cause of action for breach of an implied contract claim fails because the undisputed evidence shows that the California Nurses Association represents nurses such as Ms. Santiago and the hospital and CNA entered into a CBA. The CBA governs the parties' contractual relationship and federal law preempts Ms. Santiago's common law contract claim. (Levy v. Skywalker Sound (2003) 108 Cal.App.4th 753.). Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must be in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600, 415.551.5868, Email: torreanom@aol.com. =(302/HEK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Aug-22-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance by any party or counsel.

There being no appearances, the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants Dignity Health, Inc. and St. Francis Memorial Hospital's motion for summary judgment is granted. The undisputed evidence shows that defendants are entitled to judgment in their favor on all four of the causes of action alleged in the third amended complaint filed by plaintiff Marilyn Santiago. Ms. Santiago's first cause of action for failure to reasonably accommodate lacks merit because she has not identified any reasonable accommodation that would enable her to perform her job as a nurse. "The FEHA 'does not prohibit an employer from . . . discharging an employee with a physical or mental disability . . . where the employee, because of his or her physical or mental disability, is unable to perform his or her essential duties even with reasonable accommodations . . . .'" (Scotch v. Art Institute of California (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 986, 1005 citing Government Code 12940(a)(1).). The undisputed facts show that Ms. Santiago could not perform her essential job duties with or without a reasonable accommodation at the time her employment was terminated. Lifting was a necessary part of her nursing job. Ms. Santiago citation to the portion of the Collective Bargaining Agreement stating that efforts should be made to reduce lifting is unavailing. While finite leave can be a reasonable accommodation, a "[r]easonable accommodation does not require the employer to wait indefinitely for an employee's medical condition to be corrected . . . ." (Hanson v. Lucky Stores, Inc. (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 215, 226-27.) Ms. Santiago acknowledged that, when her employment was terminated, she had no idea when she could return to work because of the uncertainty when she would be able to do the required lifting. Her attorney's 4/28/15 e-mail, which asked for 3 months of leave, also stated that it was unclear when she could return to work. Ms. Santiago's second and third causes of action for age and disability discrimination lack merit because defendants presented a legitimate reason for the termination of Ms. Santiago's employment and Ms. Santiago did not present substantial responsive evidence of pretext. Prior to the termination of her employment, Ms. Santiago had not worked at the hospital for several months and was not on approved leave. (Part 1 of 2 of the Court's ruling)
